pair_convention.spot_explanation: NZD/USD at 0.5769 means 1 New Zealand Dollar buys 0.5769 US Dollars. A rising NZD/USD rate means the NZD is strengthening against the USD (each NZD buys more USD). A falling NZD/USD rate means the NZD is weakening against the USD (each NZD buys fewer USD). The base currency is NZD and the quote currency is USD.

executive_summary: NZD/USD trades at 0.5769, having declined 2.73% over the past month and 4.85% over the past year, currently sitting at the 44th percentile of its 52-week range of 0.5486–0.6122. The pair is dominated by a dual headwind regime: the Iran-US conflict has simultaneously strengthened the USD through safe-haven demand and weakened the NZD through NZ's acute energy import vulnerability — a 100% refined fuel importer facing Brent at $107+/bbl. The bilateral rate differential of -138bp (RBNZ 2.25% vs Fed 3.625%) generates persistent negative carry for NZD/USD longs, ranking NZD as the second-weakest G10 carry currency against the dollar. The key bilateral divergence from standalone dossier views is that the pair's sensitivity to the Iran conflict is asymmetric: NZ's lack of a Fed swap line, 100% fuel import dependency, and small/illiquid currency make NZD/USD one of the most conflict-sensitive pairs in G10, amplifying the bearish signal beyond what either dossier alone would suggest. NZ's stagflationary profile (CPI 3.1%, GDP +0.2% QoQ, unemployment 5.4%) contrasts starkly with the US's more resilient growth backdrop, and this growth differential is widening in USD's favour. Our headline stance: tactically bearish with conviction 3/5, strategically mildly bearish with conviction 2/5 — but the risk/reward is shifting toward NZD-positive at the 6-month horizon given 16% PPP undervaluation, extreme short positioning (80th–90th percentile), and dairy prices up 18% YTD.

market_context.valuation_assessment: NZD/USD is deeply undervalued by purchasing power parity metrics. The NZD dossier cites OECD PPP-implied fair value at ~0.685, representing approximately 16% undervaluation at current levels. The USD dossier's own PPP table shows NZD/USD PPP fair value at ~0.712, confirming the NZD is cheap. The BIS REER for NZD at 102.72 sits at only the 35th percentile historically, while the USD REER at 106.94 is at the 72nd percentile. The combined signal is unambiguous: NZD/USD is structurally cheap by every valuation measure, but the conflict premium, carry disadvantage, and NZ-specific vulnerabilities justify a discount — the question is whether the discount is excessive.

positioning.net_pair_implication: The combined positioning picture for NZD/USD is among the most bearishly configured in G10: specs are simultaneously net short NZD at the 80th–90th percentile and net long USD with the largest weekly buying since June 2018, creating a double-barrel bearish positioning signal that amplifies downside momentum but also creates significant squeeze risk on any positive catalyst.

market_context.recent_regime: NZD/USD has been caught in a vicious cycle since late February. The NZD fell to $0.572, its lowest level since late November, as investors weighed the RBNZ's policy outlook amid the ongoing Middle East conflict. The RBNZ has cautioned that the country is vulnerable to surging energy costs, and Governor Breman signalled that interest rates could rise if price pressures threaten longer-term inflation expectations. The pair's high-beta risk-on character has amplified the selloff relative to AUD/USD, widening AUD/NZD from ~1.16 to ~1.20 — reflecting NZ's greater oil vulnerability, lower OCR, and absence of a Fed swap line.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The bilateral carry is unambiguously negative for NZD/USD longs. The RBNZ's OCR at 2.25% versus the Fed Funds midpoint of 3.625% creates a -138bp annualised carry cost. At estimated 1M implied volatility of ~14%, the carry-to-vol ratio is approximately -0.10 — deeply negative and insufficient to attract carry-seeking capital. The RBNZ's published track projects the OCR essentially flat through mid-2026, while market pricing has pulled forward hikes to H2 2026. Even with two 25bp RBNZ hikes to 2.75% by December, the differential would only narrow to -88bp — still significantly negative. Forward points are estimated at approximately +8 pips (1M), +25 pips (3M), and +50 pips (6M), reflecting the positive NZD premium from the short-end yield pickup in certain tenors. The carry regime is clearly 'not working' for NZD/USD bulls — the combination of negative carry and risk-off USD strength makes holding NZD/USD longs expensive and painful, ranking NZD as the second-weakest G10 carry currency against the dollar.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral rate differential of -138bp (RBNZ 2.25% vs Fed 3.625%) is the widest NZD-USD gap since the RBNZ's aggressive cutting cycle began in August 2024, and it directly weighs on NZD/USD through three channels: negative carry for longs, forward points that reward shorts, and systematic flow from carry-targeting strategies. The trajectory over the next 2–4 weeks is flat-to-widening: the RBNZ is universally expected to hold at the April 8 MPR, while the Fed is firmly on hold at 3.50–3.75% with the May FOMC priced at 95% hold. OIS markets show no NZD carry improvement within the tactical horizon. The 2Y sovereign yield spread (NZ 3.58% vs US 3.89% = -31bp) understates the front-end differential, while the 10Y spread is modestly positive (NZ 4.78% vs US 4.37% = +41bp) reflecting NZ's elevated term premium from Fitch's negative outlook. The Fed's March dot plot retained one cut for 2026, but bond markets are pricing flat-to-hike, meaning the US rate floor is solid. Governor Breman's 'look through' guidance on the oil shock signals no imminent RBNZ tightening, removing any carry catalyst for NZD.

tactical.what_is_priced: The negative carry is fully reflected in NZD/USD's proximity to multi-month lows. The remaining tactical edge is small: if the April 8 MPR delivers any hawkish language shift ('concerned about' rather than 'look through'), hike pricing could modestly support NZD — but this is a low-probability outcome.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic rate picture is mildly less bearish because the direction of differential movement is toward narrowing. Market pricing implies RBNZ hikes beginning in H2 2026 (potentially reaching 2.50–2.75% by December), while the Fed dot plot projects one cut (to 3.375%). If both materialise, the differential narrows from -138bp to approximately -63bp — a significant improvement but still negative. The May 27 RBNZ MPS is the pivotal event: an upward OCR track revision would validate hike pricing and modestly support NZD via carry expectations. However, the global rate environment is not standing still: the RBA has hiked to 4.10%, Norges Bank is signalling imminent tightening, and the BoJ is normalising — meaning other currencies' carry positions are improving faster than NZD's. Even at 2.75%, NZD would remain below the Fed, BoE (3.75%), RBA (4.10+%), and Norges Bank (4.00+%), limiting the strategic carry recovery. The Warsh Fed Chair transition introduces additional uncertainty about the US rate path that could delay or accelerate differential narrowing.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: NZ's stagflationary profile (CPI 3.1%, GDP +0.2% QoQ, unemployment 5.4%) contrasts starkly with the US's more resilient growth backdrop (GDP +0.7% SAAR, ISM Manufacturing 52.4, ISM Services 56.1). While US inflation is also re-accelerating (Core PCE 3.1%), the US economy has the scale and energy self-sufficiency to absorb the oil shock far better than NZ's small, 100% fuel-importing economy. The ANZ Business Outlook collapse from 59.2 to 32.5 — the sharpest one-month decline since the pandemic — has no US equivalent. The Q1 NZ CPI release (~April 16) is expected at 3.3–3.5%, which would deepen the stagflationary narrative. On the US side, March CPI (April 10) is expected to show headline acceleration to 2.8–3.0% from oil pass-through — hawkish for the Fed and USD-supportive. The growth differential is widening in USD's favour: NZ's ASB GDP forecast has been slashed to 1.3% for 2026 while the Atlanta Fed GDPNow tracks +2.0% for Q1.

tactical.what_is_priced: The growth differential is partially reflected in NZD/USD's decline, but the upcoming data flow (NZ CPI April 16, US CPI April 10) could amplify the divergence. An NZ CPI print above 3.5% would be particularly NZD-negative.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic growth divergence depends on oil shock duration. If the conflict resolves by mid-year, NZ's pre-conflict recovery (PMI 55.0, housing stabilisation) could reassert, narrowing the growth gap. However, even in the base case, NZ's GDP is projected at 1.3–1.9% vs US 2.0%+, and NZ's stagflationary trap constrains RBNZ policy response in ways the Fed does not face. The US's relative energy independence is a structural advantage that persists regardless of conflict resolution. Over 6 months, the risk is that the NZ economy deteriorates further if oil stays elevated — ASB warns recovery is 'unlikely until 2027.' NZ's 2026 GDP forecast of 1.3% (ASB) to 1.9% (Westpac) compares unfavourably to the US at ~2.0%, and CPI is heading toward 3.5–4.0% vs US 2.4–3.0%. NZ unemployment is rising toward 5.5%+ while US at 4.4% is stabilising. The oil shock adds an estimated +1.6ppt to NZ CPI over a 1-month closure scenario vs near-zero for the energy self-sufficient US.

The growth differential is underpriced — a protracted oil shock pushing NZ toward recession (Q2 GDP contraction) is a 20–25% probability tail risk not reflected in consensus.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: NZD/USD is one of the purest risk-on/risk-off barometers in G10 — the NZD is a high-beta risk proxy while the USD is the primary safe haven. The current risk-off regime (VIX 30.6, S&P 500 -7.3% YTD, US HY OAS 321bp) places the pair firmly in the 'dual headwind' quadrant where both the NZD leg weakens (risk-off sells NZD) and the USD leg strengthens (risk-off buys USD). The dollar smile framework positions USD at the left side (risk-off strength) — the worst quadrant for NZD/USD. NZD/JPY at ~90.50 is flashing red as a risk barometer. NZD is underperforming AUD within the Antipodean complex (AUD/NZD at 1.1987) due to NZ's greater energy vulnerability and lower liquidity — a relative value signal that the market perceives NZD as the more vulnerable risk currency.

tactical.what_is_priced: The risk-off NZD/USD compression is well-priced at current levels. The unpriced risk is further escalation: Hormuz full closure → VIX 40+ → NZD/USD 0.55 or lower. Conversely, a ceasefire would trigger a violent NZD/USD rally amplified by the pair's high beta and compressed shorts.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic risk assessment is a binary function of geopolitical resolution. If the Iran conflict resolves (50% base case), risk appetite recovers, VIX normalises to 15–20, and NZD/USD benefits from both NZD risk-on rally and USD safe-haven unwind — a powerful double tailwind that could drive 300–500 pips of appreciation. If the conflict persists or escalates, the risk-off regime continues and NZD/USD retests lows. NZD's high beta works in both directions — the pair would lead the G10 rally in a risk-on recovery. Historical post-conflict risk rallies show NZD/USD appreciating 5–10% over 3–6 months following geopolitical resolution. The dollar smile base case shifts from the left side (risk-off) to the bottom (goldilocks = weak USD), the most favourable quadrant for NZD/USD. NZD beta to VIX is approximately -0.8 to -1.0, confirming high sensitivity in both directions. The neutral stance reflects the genuine 50/50 uncertainty about the geopolitical outcome over 1–6 months.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The combined positioning picture for NZD/USD is the most bearishly configured in G10: specs are simultaneously net short NZD (-41,742 contracts, 80th–90th percentile) and net long USD (+$6.3B). This double-barrel positioning amplifies the pair's downside momentum but creates equally powerful upside squeeze potential. The weekly flow dynamic shows NZD shorts still building (-3,724 last week) while USD longs saw an $11B net buying in the week to March 17 — the largest since June 2018. Tactically, the trade is consensus: the risk/reward of adding new shorts at these levels is compressed because (a) sellers are already positioned and (b) any positive catalyst triggers covering on both legs simultaneously. The neutral stance reflects the tension between positioning momentum (bearish) and crowding risk (bullish contrarian signal).

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, the extreme positioning on both legs of NZD/USD creates a compelling contrarian bullish signal. NZD shorts at the 80th–90th percentile have historically preceded positioning unwinds within 3–6 months. The USD's rapid build to +$6.3B is conflict-driven and temporary — once the geopolitical premium fades, these positions reverse. Asset manager NZD shorts (-25,012) are the slower-to-unwind category, meaning when covering begins, the flow is sustained over weeks. NZD's small open interest means even modest covering ($500M–$1B) drives meaningful spot appreciation. The probability of a significant positioning-driven NZD/USD rally within 6 months is 55–60%. The combined unwind on both legs — shorts covering NZD AND longs selling USD simultaneously — would be amplified, contributing an estimated 150–300 pips to NZD/USD recovery beyond fundamental drivers alone.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: NZ and the US have markedly different commodity exposures, creating an asymmetric oil shock impact. NZ is a 100% refined fuel importer with only 52 days of reserves, while the US is roughly energy self-sufficient through shale production. Applying the commodity direction rules: oil ↑ → mildly neutral for USD (self-sufficient) but sharply negative for NZD (100% importer). Net effect: oil ↑ is bearish NZD/USD. However, dairy prices (++NZD, neutral USD) provide an offsetting force: WMP at $3,709/MT and +18% YTD represents significant improvement. The bilateral trade relationship is modest but growing — US goods trade with NZ totalled approximately $9.7 billion in 2025, and the US has overtaken Australia to become NZ's second-largest export market after China. The NZ-US current account differential (NZ -3.5% vs US -2.4%) is modestly bearish NZD/USD but not decisive. The net commodity exposure is roughly balanced tactically — oil negative offsets dairy positive — resulting in a neutral stance.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the external balance picture shifts NZD/USD-bullish because the dairy rally is structural while the oil shock is assumed temporary. NZ's dairy export dominance (~25–30% of merchandise exports) and the GDT rally (+18% YTD, 6 consecutive increases) create a durable support floor. Fonterra's lifted milk price forecast (NZ$9.20–9.80/kgMS) signals confidence. NZ's current account improvement from -9.0% to -3.5% of GDP is underappreciated structural de-risking. Once oil normalises to $80–90/bbl, NZ's terms of trade revert to the improving trajectory (+3.7% QoQ in Q4 2025). The US twin deficits (C/A -2.4% + fiscal -5.8%) require persistent foreign capital inflows that are increasingly competed for by higher-yielding alternatives — structurally USD-negative. The India-NZ FTA (concluded December 2025) opens new export market potential.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral guidance divergence is bearish for NZD/USD. The Fed's March FOMC delivered a hawkish hold with the dot plot retaining one cut but shifting the distribution hawkish (14/19 dots at 0–1 cuts). Powell's conditional guidance ('if we don't see progress, then you won't see that rate cut') signals no imminent easing. The RBNZ under Governor Breman is explicitly dovish — the 'look through' guidance on the oil shock means no carry support from rate hikes. Following Breman's comments, markets trimmed bets on an imminent move, with traders now pricing around a 50% chance of a rate hike in May, down from 68% before her remarks. The April 8 RBNZ MPR (no new forecasts) and April 8 FOMC minutes create a concentrated communication window where the bilateral guidance gap is likely to be reinforced. The market-RBNZ gap (market prices hikes that the RBNZ hasn't endorsed) creates uncertainty rather than clarity for NZD.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The USD liquidity environment is hostile for NZD/USD. The Iran conflict has triggered safe-haven USD demand (DXY ~100.5, +2.8% in March), tightening global dollar conditions for energy-importing nations that need more USD to pay for oil at $107+/bbl. SOFR is well-behaved at 3.63% (no money market stress), and QT ended in December 2025 — but the conflict-driven USD demand overwhelms these positives. Critically, the RBNZ does NOT have a standing Fed swap line, unlike the RBA, ECB, BoJ, BoE, and SNB. This means in a USD liquidity crisis, NZ institutions face higher funding costs than their peers — a structural vulnerability unique to NZD. NZ's current account deficit (-3.5% of GDP) requires persistent foreign capital inflows, and tighter USD liquidity raises this cost. The dollar smile's risk-off quadrant directly targets NZD/USD as one of the pair's most vulnerable to safe-haven flows.

tactical.rationale: The tactical bearish case for NZD/USD is driven by convergence across the three highest-weighted factors: Policy & Rates (-0.168), Inflation & Growth (-0.126), and Risk Appetite (-0.098) — together accounting for 69% of the total bearish score. The -138bp carry differential is the widest since the RBNZ's cutting cycle began and generates a persistent drag with no improvement catalyst within 2–4 weeks. NZ's stagflationary trap (CPI 3.1%, GDP +0.2%, unemployment 5.4%) contrasts sharply with US resilience, and the oil shock operates through NZ's 100% fuel import dependency with disproportionate force. Risk-off conditions (VIX 30.6) activate NZD/USD's high beta in the bearish direction while the dollar smile places USD at the risk-off left side. The Iran conflict creates an asymmetric bilateral impact — NZ's lack of a Fed swap line and small/illiquid currency amplify the pair's vulnerability beyond what either standalone dossier captures. The RBNZ's 'look through' guidance removes any near-term carry catalyst and the Fed's hawkish hold reinforces the bilateral guidance gap. Event risk is unambiguously bearish with the April 6 Hormuz deadline as a binary pressure point, and NZD/USD's technical structure confirms the bear trend with spot below all three major moving averages. Conviction is limited to 3/5 rather than 4/5 by two offsetting factors: extreme short positioning (-41,742 NZD combined CFTC contracts at 80th–90th percentile) compresses risk/reward for new shorts and creates asymmetric squeeze risk; and dairy prices up 18% YTD provide a structural commodity floor. The trade is consensus, and NZD is 16% below PPP — deeply cheap on any multi-month view. The path of least resistance is lower absent a geopolitical catalyst, with the April 6 Hormuz deadline as the binary event that could generate ±200 pips.

Fourth, NZ election risk (November 7) adds late-year uncertainty to NZD that is entirely absent from most bank forecasts. BNZ's own research is most aligned with our view, noting the conflict is 'a major geopolitical event that should not be under-estimated' and that risk is 'heavily skewed to the downside,' though they stop short of revising forecasts formally.

De-escalation (oil -20%, VIX -10pts) would trigger NZD/USD +200–300 pips through the reverse mechanism — NZD risk-on rally combined with USD safe-haven unwind. The pair's high beta to risk and NZ's oil dependency make this the most impactful single event for NZD/USD in the tactical window, dominating all other factors.

A seventh consecutive GDT price increase would extend the dairy rally that provides NZD's structural commodity floor. WMP above $3,700/MT is the bullish threshold — confirming the +18% YTD rally has momentum. This catalyst affects only the NZD base leg (USD is insensitive to dairy) and would provide relief in the context of the broader risk-off environment. A sharp decline of 5%+ would compound NZD weakness by removing the key offset to the oil shock narrative and potentially triggering additional NZD selling.

catalysts.event: RBNZ MPR Release + FOMC March Meeting Minutes (Same Day)
catalysts.rationale: The same-day release creates a concentrated bilateral communication event. The RBNZ hold is expected with dovish 'look through' language, providing no NZD carry support — markets may trim the 50% May hike probability if language is more accommodative than expected. Simultaneously, FOMC minutes from the hawkish March meeting may reveal internal debate about resuming hikes rather than cutting, strengthening the USD's rate advantage narrative. The same-day divergence — dovish RBNZ versus hawkish Fed minutes — could amplify the bilateral guidance gap and push NZD/USD lower by 50–100 pips.

Oil pass-through from Brent at $107+/bbl is expected to push US headline CPI toward 2.8–3.0% in March from 2.4% in February. A hawkish reading above 3.0% would collapse June Fed cut probability (currently priced at partial), solidifying the Fed's higher-for-longer stance and strengthening the USD's carry advantage over the NZD. This is a quote-leg catalyst that widens the bilateral carry differential by reinforcing the Fed on hold narrative.

Consensus expects 3.3–3.5% YoY, up from 3.1%, driven by oil pass-through and services inflation. A print above 3.5% deepens the stagflation narrative — supply-shock inflation that the RBNZ cannot address without risking recession. This is NZD-negative because it simultaneously constrains RBNZ policy space and signals deteriorating real purchasing power. The only bullish scenario is a sub-3.0% surprise that signals the oil shock hasn't yet passed through to broader prices, which would partially validate the 'look through' approach.

A BoJ hike would compress UST-JGB spreads and weaken USD broadly through the global rate convergence narrative, indirectly supporting NZD/USD through a weaker USD channel. However, the direct NZD impact is secondary — this primarily affects NZD/JPY and USD/JPY rather than NZD/USD directly. The ~30% hike probability priced means a hike would modestly support NZD/USD while a hold would be marginally USD-supportive. The indirect channel is insufficient to override the pair's dominant bilateral drivers.

catalysts.event: FOMC May Meeting (Potentially Powell's Last as Chair)
catalysts.rationale: The May FOMC brings a fresh SEP (Summary of Economic Projections) with updated dot plot. If hawkish (dots shift to no cuts in 2026), USD strengthens and NZD/USD faces renewed pressure. If dovish (Chair transition gesture or oil-driven growth concern), USD weakens and NZD/USD benefits through the quote leg. The Warsh confirmation status at this meeting determines its character — an unconstrained Powell final meeting may differ from one shaped by transition dynamics. The ~95% hold probability is priced; the risk is in the dots and language.

catalysts.event: RBNZ Monetary Policy Statement (MPS) with Full OCR Track Revision
catalysts.rationale: This is the most important domestic event for NZD/USD over the next 6 months. The first full RBNZ forecast update incorporating the oil shock, Fitch downgrade, and confidence collapse will either validate or invalidate market hike pricing. A materially higher OCR track (signalling hikes in H2 2026) is NZD-positive through carry improvement expectations and signals inflation-fighting credibility. An unchanged or lower track (RBNZ validates 'look through' and resists market pricing) is NZD-negative, potentially triggering 100–200 pip selling as hike premium is unwound. This is the strategic inflection point identified throughout the analysis.

The pre-election Budget from Finance Minister Willis must balance fiscal credibility (addressing Fitch's negative outlook) with election spending pressures. An expansionary budget with wider projected deficits risks a Fitch downgrade to AA and is NZD-negative through sovereign risk repricing. A consolidation budget with credible surplus path is NZD-positive by addressing the negative outlook but is politically risky ahead of the November election. The day-after combination with the RBNZ MPS creates a 24-hour period of maximum NZD repricing risk.

Knife-edge race between Centre-right (current government) and Labour-Green coalition. Centre-right continuity is NZD-neutral to mildly positive (policy continuity, fiscal discipline). Labour-Green victory would be initially NZD-negative through CGT uncertainty and higher spending plans. NZ First as potential kingmaker adds significant unpredictability to the outcome. Election uncertainty typically adds 1–2% NZD risk premium in the 6–8 weeks prior to polling. This is the longest-dated catalyst in the strategic horizon and is entirely absent from current bank consensus forecasts.

invalidators.condition: Brent crude falls below $85/bbl and sustains for 2+ weeks (10+ consecutive trading days)
invalidators.mechanism: NZ's primary vulnerability (100% fuel import dependency) would be substantially defused at $85/bbl, compressing the conflict risk premium embedded in NZD/USD, making the 16% PPP undervaluation actionable and potentially triggering short covering on both legs
invalidators.monitor_indicator: ICE Brent front-month continuous contract daily close

invalidators.condition: NZD/USD closes above the 200-day SMA (0.5830) for 3 consecutive sessions
invalidators.mechanism: The bearish trend structure would be broken, triggering CTA and systematic short-covering as the key trend-defining level is reclaimed; this removes the systematic selling pressure that has reinforced the bear trend and signals a potential trend reversal

invalidators.condition: RBNZ hikes the OCR by 25bp or more at any scheduled meeting
invalidators.mechanism: An RBNZ hike signals inflation-fighting credibility, narrows the bilateral carry differential by at least 25bp, validates market hike pricing that the RBNZ has not yet endorsed, and likely triggers a short squeeze as the carry disadvantage narrative is challenged; the 'look through' position would be abandoned
invalidators.monitor_indicator: RBNZ OCR announcement per meeting schedule

invalidators.condition: Combined CFTC NZD net short position exceeds -55,000 contracts
invalidators.mechanism: Extreme net short beyond -55,000 indicates the short trade has further structural support and the contrarian signal is overwhelmed by fundamental momentum; at this level, positioning is no longer a headwind to the bearish thesis and the downside move has further to run
invalidators.monitor_indicator: CFTC Commitment of Traders weekly report — NZD combined net position

invalidators.condition: NZD/USD breaks below 0.5486 (52-week low) on a daily closing basis
invalidators.mechanism: The technical breakdown below the key annual support triggers systematic selling from momentum and trend-following strategies, removes the last major support level, and signals the market is pricing a more severe NZ economic outcome than consensus; the absence of nearby support to 0.5300 implies additional downside velocity

signposts.confirms_if: Brent stays above $100/bbl (oil shock persists, NZ vulnerability active, pair bearish thesis confirmed)
signposts.invalidates_if: Brent falls below $85/bbl (conflict risk premium compresses, NZD/USD recovery triggered)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The single most important bilateral signpost for NZD/USD — NZ's 100% refined fuel import dependency with only 52 days of reserves creates extreme sensitivity to oil prices, while the US is roughly energy self-sufficient through shale production. No other G10 pair has this degree of one-sided oil vulnerability, making Brent the primary amplifier of the bilateral bearish signal.

signposts.indicator: GDT Dairy Price Index (Whole Milk Powder)
signposts.confirms_if: WMP falls below $3,400/MT (dairy support breaks, removing the primary NZD structural floor)
signposts.invalidates_if: WMP rises above $4,000/MT (dairy tailwind dominates oil headwind, NZD/USD floor strengthens materially)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Dairy is NZ's principal export (~25–30% of merchandise exports) and the primary NZD-specific commodity driver; the USD has no material dairy export sensitivity. The GDT rally (+18% YTD, 6 consecutive increases) is the key offset to the oil shock — monitoring dairy prices captures the bilateral terms of trade differential that is unique to the NZD leg.

signposts.confirms_if: Spot remains below 0.5830 for 3+ consecutive sessions (bearish trend intact, systematic shorts maintained)
signposts.invalidates_if: Three consecutive daily closes above 0.5830 (trend broken, CTA short-covering triggered, bear thesis invalidated)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 200d SMA is the critical trend-defining level for this pair — the MA separates the bearish trend regime (all systematic and momentum capital positioned short) from a potential recovery phase. A sustained break above would trigger an estimated 150–200 pip short-covering move from CTAs alone, amplified by NZD's small OI and the concentrated short positioning.

signposts.confirms_if: VIX stays above 25 (risk-off persists, NZD/USD's high beta continues to amplify the bearish signal)
signposts.invalidates_if: VIX falls below 18 (risk normalises, NZD/USD recovery triggered through both legs simultaneously)
signposts.bilateral_importance: NZD/USD is one of the highest-beta G10 risk proxy pairs with NZD beta to VIX approximately -0.8 to -1.0. VIX directly determines the pair's regime — above 25 places the pair in the dual-headwind quadrant (NZD weakens + USD strengthens), while below 18 would flip to the goldilocks quadrant (NZD rallies + USD weakens). No other single indicator captures the bilateral risk dynamic as efficiently.

signposts.indicator: CFTC Net Speculative NZD Positioning (Combined Leveraged + Asset Manager)
signposts.confirms_if: Net short reduces toward -20K without a corresponding NZD spot rally (indicates fundamental selling rather than covering, extending the bear trend)
signposts.invalidates_if: Net short exceeds -55K (extreme positioning preceding a sharp positioning-driven reversal) or rapidly covers above -20K (short squeeze in progress)
signposts.bilateral_importance: NZD's small open interest (65,011 contracts) means positioning extremes are reached at lower absolute levels than major currencies, amplifying the signal. The combined short at -41,742 represents an unusually large fraction of total OI, and combined with the USD's conflict-driven long (+$6.3B), creates the most bearishly configured NZD/USD positioning in recent years. Mean-reversion from extreme positioning drives price action independent of fundamentals.

signposts.current_level: 39.3 (March 2026; down from 59.2 in January — sharpest monthly drop since pandemic)
signposts.confirms_if: Falls below 30 (confidence collapse deepens, growth differential widens further, RBNZ under more stagflationary pressure)
signposts.invalidates_if: Recovers above 50 (pre-conflict recovery trajectory reasserts, NZD growth narrative improves)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The ANZ Business Outlook is a NZ-specific confidence indicator with no USD equivalent, directly capturing the oil shock's real economy impact on the NZD leg. The collapse from 59.2 to 32.5 has no parallel in US indicators (ISM composite remains expansionary), making this the purest measure of the bilateral growth divergence that is widening in USD's favour.

assumptions: Carry calculation uses the RBNZ OCR (2.25%) and Fed Funds midpoint (3.625%) as the bilateral rate differential proxy. Exact market forward points are estimated, not observed from live OTC data.

PPP valuations use the NZD dossier's OECD 2024 PPP estimate of 0.685 and the USD dossier's 0.712 estimate. Both confirm deep NZD undervaluation but the exact magnitude depends on methodology and base year selection.
Implied volatility estimates (~14% 1M) are from the NZD dossier, derived from the VIX-NZD/USD IV historical relationship. Exact OTC market data was not available at time of analysis.

Risk reversal estimates (negative, NZD puts bid over calls) are inferred from positioning and spot dynamics rather than actual OTC market data — directional accuracy is assumed but magnitude is approximate.
Positioning percentiles use a 3-year lookback from the NZD dossier. The small NZD OI means percentile extremes are reached at lower absolute levels than major currencies.

Bank forecast consensus is compiled from pre-conflict forecasts by NAB, MUFG, Westpac, TD Securities, ING, and BNZ. Most have not been formally updated for the Iran conflict. BNZ's March 13 research note signals downside risk awareness but stops short of revising forecasts.

Weight adjustments reflect NZD/USD's classification as a high-beta risk proxy pair, justifying a +0.03 tactical boost to Factor 3 (Risk Appetite) funded by -0.01 each from Factors 8 and 9, and a +0.02 strategic boost to Factor 3 funded by -0.01 each from Factors 8 and 9.

The Iran conflict base case assumes de-escalation by mid-2026 (assigned 50% probability) based on historical analogue duration, Trump's ceasefire signals, and domestic political pressure from 33% approval ratings and rising US gas prices.
The RBNZ 'look through' guidance interpretation assumes Governor Breman maintains this stance through the April 8 MPR; any deviation would be a surprise that is not reflected in the tactical stance.

USD aggregate speculative positioning figure of +$6.3B is from the week to March 17, 2026 — the most recent CFTC data available at time of writing. More recent data may show additional USD long accumulation or early unwinding.
